relevant_files.role: Main security scanner entry point - handles diff and file scanning
14 regex patterns for vulnerability detection (hardcoded secrets, SQL injection, XSS, etc.)
Core types: Finding, PatternDefinition, Severity, Confidence
LLM-based validation to reduce false positives
Integration tests including multi-line limitation test
Package manifest - dependencies to be extended with tree-sitter

conventions.license_headers: SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0 on all source files
conventions.error_handling: Uses thiserror for custom errors, anyhow for applications
conventions.async_patterns: Uses tokio for async runtime, async-trait for trait bounds
conventions.testing: Unit tests in same file under #[cfg(test)], integration tests in tests/
conventions.builder_pattern: Uses bon crate for builder generation
conventions.serialization: Uses serde with derive macros, snake_case naming

patterns.context: SecurityScanner uses regex patterns loaded from patterns.json
patterns.assessment: Simple and fast, but cannot detect multi-line vulnerabilities

patterns.context: Scanner processes each line independently for regex matching
patterns.assessment: Performance trade-off that limits detection capabilities

patterns.context: SecurityValidator uses pattern confidence when LLM fails
patterns.assessment: Robust error handling with graceful degradation

patterns.context: Patterns have Low/Medium/High confidence to tune false positive rates
patterns.assessment: Good separation of detection sensitivity from severity

anti_patterns.context: Source on one line, sink on another cannot be detected
anti_patterns.assessment: Critical limitation documented in issue #735, test #736

related_issues.summary: Evaluate tree-sitter for AST-based vulnerability detection to enable multi-line pattern detection
Add negative test documenting multi-line detection limitation (CLOSED)

constraints: Must maintain backward compatibility with existing regex patterns
Each language needs its own grammar crate (5-10 languages = binary size increase)
Tree-sitter queries use Scheme-like syntax (learning curve)
Data-flow/taint tracking still requires custom logic on top of AST
Hybrid approach recommended - regex for simple patterns, tree-sitter for multi-line
14 existing patterns need evaluation for conversion to tree-sitter queries
test_coverage: Unit tests in each security module file (types, patterns, validator, detection)
Integration tests in tests/security_integration.rs with 4 test cases
Pattern matching tests for hardcoded secrets, SQL injection, weak crypto, XSS
All tests pass with cargo test -p aptu-core --test security_integration

library_findings.name: tree-sitter
library_findings.role: Core parser library - generates AST from source code
library_findings.notes: Version 0.26.5 available on crates.io. Incremental parsing support. Well-maintained by GitHub.

library_findings.name: tree-sitter-rust
library_findings.notes: Version 0.23 suggested in issue. Provides Rust AST nodes and query capabilities.

library_findings.name: tree-sitter-python
library_findings.notes: Needed for Python security pattern detection (patterns.json targets .py files)

library_findings.name: tree-sitter-javascript
library_findings.notes: Needed for XSS detection patterns targeting .js, .ts, .jsx, .tsx files

library_findings.name: tree-sitter-java
library_findings.notes: Needed for SQL injection patterns targeting .java files

library_findings.name: tree-sitter-loader
library_findings.notes: Alternative to compile-time grammar linking - reduces binary size but adds runtime complexity

approaches.title: Spike: Single-Language Tree-sitter Proof of Concept
approaches.summary: Add tree-sitter-rust only, implement one multi-line pattern (SQL injection), evaluate complexity and performance. Keep existing regex system unchanged.
pros: Small binary size impact (one grammar only)
cons: Only solves problem for Rust initially
Requires follow-up work for other languages
approaches.complexity: Low - focused scope, single language, one pattern
files_to_touch: crates/aptu-core/Cargo.toml - add tree-sitter and tree-sitter-rust deps

approaches.title: Hybrid Regex + AST Scanner
approaches.summary: Refactor SecurityScanner to use both regex (fast, simple patterns) and tree-sitter (multi-line, language-aware). Patterns marked with 'ast: true' in JSON use tree-sitter.
pros: Best of both worlds: speed for simple patterns, power for complex ones
Backward compatible - existing patterns work unchanged
Gradual migration path - convert patterns one at a time
cons: More complex codebase with two scanning systems
Need to coordinate findings from both scanners
Configuration complexity (which patterns use which scanner)
approaches.complexity: Medium - requires scanner orchestration, pattern metadata extension

approaches.title: Full Tree-sitter Migration with Query DSL
approaches.summary: Replace all regex patterns with tree-sitter queries. Create domain-specific query language for security patterns that compiles to tree-sitter queries.
pros: Maximum detection capability for multi-line vulnerabilities
Unified query syntax across all languages
cons: High migration cost - rewrite all 14 patterns
Significant binary size increase (5-10 grammar crates)
Performance overhead of full AST parsing vs regex
Risk of regressions in detection accuracy
approaches.complexity: High - full rewrite of pattern system, extensive testing needed
files_to_touch: crates/aptu-core/src/security/patterns.rs - replace regex with query execution
All pattern tests need rewrite for new query syntax

recommendation: Approach 2 (Hybrid Regex + AST Scanner) is recommended. The current regex system works well for simple patterns like hardcoded secrets and weak crypto that appear on single lines. A hybrid approach lets us keep these fast paths while adding tree-sitter specifically for multi-line vulnerabilities like SQL injection where source and sink are separated. This minimizes risk, maintains backward compatibility, and allows gradual migration. Start with Approach 1 (Spike) to validate the tree-sitter integration, then proceed to full hybrid implementation.
